search_result.title: Oscar winner 'Flow,' Latvian animated film, makes history with win
search_result.text:
Danielle Broadway

Reuters

Updated March 2, 2025, 11:53 p.m. ET

LOS ANGELES — The independent film "Flow" won the best animated feature film at the 2025 Oscars on Sunday, securing the first Academy Award for Latvia and its Latvian director Gints Zilbalodis.

"Flow" follows a cat that finds refuge on a boat along with other animals that join together after a flood destroys their homes.

The movie stood out in 2024, having been rendered on a free and open-source software platform called Blender and having no dialogue.

"I'm really moved by the warm reception our film has had. I hope this will open doors to independent animation filmmakers around the world," Zilbalodis said during his acceptance speech.

"This is the first time a film from Latvia has ever been nominated, so it really means a lot to us. We are very inspired, and we hope to be back soon."

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

"Flow" rode a tidal wave into the Oscar race after big studio films like "Inside Out 2," "Moana 2" and "The Wild Robot," which all had higher box-office numbers, lost the Golden Globe to the small independent film.

The "Flow" team also transformed its smaller budget into a nominee for the best international film Oscar, achieving rare recognition for an animated film in two major categories and making history with the first Oscar nominations for the Northern European country ever.

The 97th Academy Awards saw critical darlings and fan-favorites such as "Anora," "Conclave," "The Substance" "Dune: Part Two" and "Wicked" duke it out for the year's top honors.

"Anora," an indie romantic dramedy from director Sean Baker, cleaned up with five awards, including best picture, director and actress for Mikey Madison. Meanwhile, Adrien Brody took home his second best actor Oscar for "The Brutalist," the historical epic that picked up three wins.

Additionally, "Succession" star Kieran Culkin snagged best supporting actor for "A Real Pain" while Zoe Saldaña got best supporting actress for "Emilia Pérez."

Contributing: Brian Truitt, USA TODAY

search_result.title: Oscar-nominated movies you can stream at home right now
search_result.text:
Feb. 28, 2025 Updated March 23, 2025, 9:41 a.m. ET

Love movies? Live for TV? USA TODAY's Watch Party newsletter has all the best recommendations, delivered right to your inbox. Sign up now and be one of the cool kids.

You've only got a couple days left in that most important part of any Oscar season: watching all the Academy Awards-nominated movies before the big night!

With the exception of best picture contender "I'm Still Here" (which is still in theaters), most have made their way to streaming services or are available to purchase or rent. (In fact, the movie with the most Oscar nods, "Emilia Pérez," is a Netflix original.) That means whether you're a hardcore cinephile or a casual movie fan who loves doing Oscar pools, you can have quite the film festival from the comfort of your couch.

Here are 18 nominated movies to watch at home right now:

'Emilia Pérez'

There's been no hotter (or more divisive) movie this Oscar season, but Hollywood is on Emilia's side, considering the raft of nominations for this Spanish-language mix of a melodramatic telenovela, pulpy crime thriller and movie musical. After a Golden Globe win for best comedy/musical, a best picture nod was assumed. But the momentum kept up from there, with two tunes making the best original song cut, trans actress Karla Sofía Gascón scoring a history-making nomination, and Zoe Saldaña being a front-runner in the supporting actress race.

Where to watch: Netflix.

'The Brutalist'

You don't score 10 Oscar nominations without being pretty darn monumental, and that's director Brady Corbet's historical epic in a nutshell, from gorgeous music score and production design to its expansive narrative. A Hungarian Jewish architect (Adrien Brody) finds a job in postwar America, tries to bring his wife (Felicity Jones) over from Europe, and weathers his own ego and vices in a movie that takes a hard look at the immigrant experience and what happens when the "American dream" is held just out of arm's length.

Where to watch:Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Wicked'

"Popular" isn't just a show tune in the big and showy Broadway adaptation, it's a reality for the blockbuster with a best picture nomination. Jon M. Chu's bewitching "Wizard of Oz" musical prequel conjured 10 nominations, with many coming in the technical categories like costume and makeup. Most significantly, the film's two standout leads popped in the acting categories: Cynthia Erivo's powerfully rousing performance as outsider Elphaba is worth that best actress entry, while Ariana Grande earned a supporting actress nod as the hair-whipping, doe-eyed Glinda.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'A Complete Unknown'

Whether or not Timothée Chalamet pulls off a best actor victory, all the training he did to sing, play guitar and blow on a harmonica was worth it to see him masterfully play Bob Dylan in the electric biopic. "Unknown" scored eight nods total, including best picture, supporting actress (Monica Barbaro as Joan Baez) and best director (James Mangold), and it's the kind of flick music fans – and Oscar voters – love, chronicling a young Dylan's rise in the early 1960s folk music scene of New York City.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Conclave'

The fact that this papal thriller played well for Oscar voters is not a surprise: Director Edward Berger mixes locked-room mystery, courtroom drama, detective tale and political thriller for a recipe just made for best picture, one of its eight total nominations. Ralph Fiennes got his third career Oscar nomination (and second for best actor) for his role as a stressed-out but good-hearted cardinal leading the meeting to determine the next pope, while Isabella Rossellini received her first-ever nomination in supporting actress for playing a helpful nun.

Where to watch: Peacock, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Anora'

Like a millennial "Pretty Woman," the stripper dramedy found kudos among critics and some definite love among Oscar voters. In addition to best picture, filmmaker Sean Baker received directing and original screenplay nominations, Mikey Madison's hot-tempered and emotional portrayal of a Cinderella-esque sex worker made her a best actress shoo-in, and breakout Yura Borisov nabbed a deserved slot in supporting actor for playing the most interesting cinematic henchman in recent memory.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'The Substance'

This bonkers and bloody body-horror spectacle snagging five nominations (including best picture) is a win in itself. Demi Moore earned her first best actress nomination for her crazypants role as an aging celebrity who takes a treatment that unlocks her younger self (Margaret Qualley). French filmmaker Coralie Fargeat is the ninth woman to get a best director nomination and also scored an original screenplay nod, and the film totally deserves that best makeup Oscar nod for the creation of the monstrously meme-worthy Monstro Elisasue.

Where to watch:MUBI, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Dune: Part Two'

Once you see Timothée Chalamet do his music thing, Bob Dylan go watch his other best picture nominee. Denis Villeneuve's epic sci-fi sequel, which features Chalamet as the messianic Paul Atreides, is a sprawling, sandworm-filled triumph that snagged five Oscar nods, including best cinematography, sound, production design and visual effects.

Where to watch: Max, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Nickel Boys'

Up for best picture and adapted screenplay, director RaMell Ross’ innovative drama tells its riveting tale from the first-person vantage of its two young protagonists. In 1960s Jim Crow Florida, Black teenager Elwood (Ethan Herisse) has his college dreams derailed when he’s unjustly convicted of car theft and sent to reform school. He befriends fellow student Turner (Brandon Wilson) as they cope with cruel abuse and racist corruption.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Sing Sing'

Colman Domingo earned a best actor nomination last year for "Rustin" and is back in the category for this unforgettable prison drama based on a true story. Incarcerated for a crime he didn't commit, Divine G (Domingo) recruits a hardened fellow convict (Clarence Maclin) into the prisoner theatrical troupe that brings him creative joy. As great a tale as that is, it's made even better by the casting of actual thespians from Sing Sing.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'The Apprentice'

While the Donald Trump biopic didn't make much noise at the box office in the run-up to the 2024 election, a couple of major Oscar nominations likely bring some more eyeballs to the controversial drama. Sebastian Stan, who also had a fantastic Golden Globe-winning turn in "A Different Man," transformed himself into a younger version of the president to get his first best actor nomination, while "Succession" Emmy winner Jeremy Strong is in the supporting actor contingent for his unsettling role as Trump mentor Roy Cohn.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'A Real Pain'

Jesse Eisenberg's moving and hilarious dramedy, about two estranged cousins exploring their Jewish roots on a trip to Poland, didn't make the cut for best picture. However, Eisenberg took home his second Oscar nomination (and first in a non-acting category) for original screenplay. And Kieran Culkin is the highlight of the piece, absolutely terrific in a mercurial and emotional role, and the Golden Globe and Screen Actors Guild winner is the clear favorite to win best supporting actor.

Where to watch: Hulu, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'September 5'

Nominated for original screenplay, the true-life journalism thriller takes place over 24 intense hours at the 1972 Munich Olympics. When a terrorist group takes members of the Israeli team hostage in the Olympic village, the ABC Sports TV broadcast team – including rookie producer Geoff Mason (John Magaro) and his boss, Roone Arledge (Peters Sarsgaard) – scramble to cover the crisis, haggle for airtime and get the story right.

Where to watch: Paramount+, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Nosferatu'

Director Robert Eggers' remake of the horror classic drips cool 19th-century visuals and vampire glory, and it wound up in four technical Oscar races to befit such a sumptuous watch. "Nosferatu" took a bite out of the cinematography category, the vintage attire was honored with a costume design nod, Count Orlok's castle helped in earning that production design nomination, and the impressive effort to turn Bill Skarsgård into an undead mustached menace was a no-brainer for best makeup and hairstyling.

Where to watch: Peacock, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Better Man'

The eccentric music biopic/jukebox musical is up for a best visual effects Oscar for its impressive efforts turning British pop star Robbie Williams into an electric, cheeky ape. Even with signature tropes of its ilk and the career-tanking vices of many a “Behind the Music” episode, the film's unconventional storytelling methods match the fantastical nature of Williams' bananas rise-and-fall-and-rise-again tale.

Where to watch: Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Flow'

This worldless, animal-filled gem is not only going for best animated feature but it's also in contention for international film (as Latvia's entry). When a curious cat's home is wiped out by a flood, he goes on an epic quest for dry land with a capybara, a lemur, a bird, and a dog in a story with critter adventure and an ecological bent.

Where to watch: Max, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'The Wild Robot'

The family-friendly adventure was a shoo-in for best animated feature alongside Pixar's "Inside Out 2" (available on Disney+). "Wild Robot," about a robot (voiced by Lupita Nyong'o) who becomes the adoptive mother of a gosling and protector of a forest full of animals, also garnered nominations for original score and sound.

Where to watch: Peacock, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

'Inside Out 2'

Pixar's first "Inside Out" won the Oscar for best animated feature in 2016 and its enchanting sequel is going for gold as well. The jittery Anxiety (voiced by Maya Hawke) leads a mutiny and kicks out Joy (Amy Poehler) and Co. in a matured narrative that ambitiously captures what it's like for a kid (and adults) to feel overwhelmed and out of control.

Where to watch:Disney+, Apple TV, Amazon, Fandango at Home

Every recommendation is independently chosen by our editors. Purchases you make through our links may earn us a commission.

search_result.title: How to watch the 2025 Oscars from Nevada this Sunday
search_result.text:
Feb. 25, 2025 Updated Feb. 26, 2025, 4:16 p.m. ET

The highly anticipated Oscars 2025 are finally here this Sunday, March 2. This year's nominees feature wildly different films, from a biopic of Bob Dylan ("A Complete Unknown") to a film following the plot of a sex worker who marries Russian royalty ("Anora").

Here's a look at when the Oscars are, how to watch them from Nevada and what films are nominated for each category.

What day are the Oscars 2025?

The 97th Academy Awards will air at 4 p.m. PST on Sunday, March 2.

Where will the Oscars be streaming in Nevada?

You can watch the Oscars live on ABC, Hulu, YouTubeTV, AT&T TV and FuboTV.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Who is hosting the Oscars?

Conan O'Brien will be the host of the 2025 Oscars.

What is nominated for Best Picture?

"Anora"

"The Brutalist"

"A Complete Unknown"

"Conclave"

"Dune: Part Two"

"Emilia Pérez"

"I'm Still Here"

"Nickel Boys"

"The Substance"

"Wicked"

Who won Best Picture at the Oscars last year, 2024?

"Oppenheimer," a film about the creation of the atomic bomb, won Best Picture at the Oscars last year.

Best director nominees Oscars 2025

Sean Baker for "Anora"

Brady Corbet for "The Brutalist"

James Mangold for "A Complete Unknown"

Jacques Audiard for "Emilia Pérez"

Coralie Fargeat for "The Substance"

Who won best director last year Oscars 2024?

Christopher Nolan won the award for "Best Director" during the Oscars 2024 awards show.

Best actress nominees for Oscars 2025

Cynthia Erivo in "Wicked"

Karla Sofía Gascón in "Emilia Pérez"

Mikey Madison in "Anora"

Demi Moore in "The Substance"

Fernanda Torres in "I'm Still Here"

Who won Best Actress last year, Oscars 2024?

Emma Stone won Best Actress at last year's Oscars for her performance in "Poor Things," the story of a woman who is brought back to life with the brain of an infant.

Best Actor nominees for Oscars 2025

Adrien Brody for "The Brutalist"

Timothée Chalamet in "A Complete Unknown"

Colman Domingo in "Sing Sing"

Ralph Fiennes in "Conclave"

Sebastian Stan in "The Apprentice"

Who won Best Actor last year, Oscars 2024?

Cillian Murphy won Best Actor last year for his performance in "Oppenheimer."

Carly Sauvageau contributed to this report.

search_result.title: On the Academy Awards, 2025 | DAVID MURDOCK - Gadsden Times
search_result.text:
David Murdock

Contributor

Feb. 27, 2025, 4:45 a.m. ET

It’s that time of year again — the Academy Awards will be awarded on March 2. Since this is the last column of mine published beforehand, it’s the one in which I have made my predictions for the winners several times over the years. And, since I have never predicted a winner … in any category … I’m not even going to try this year.

It’s a good year for me not to try. Looking over the list of nominees for the various categories, I see only one movie from 2024 that I actually saw: “Dune: Part Two.” Unlike all my fellow fans of Frank Herbert’s class science fiction novel “Dune” out there (and fans of science fiction movies in general), I didn’t think it was all that great. A really good movie, yes, but I simply didn’t much like the take that Denis Villeneuve had on the material.

Does “Dune: Part Two” deserve a nod for Best Picture? I don’t know. Despite all the breathless reviews, I haven’t seen a single one of the other nine movies nominated. Not particularly interested in seeing any of them now, either.

Long ago, I gave up on the notion of going to see movies in movie theaters just for the sake of seeing movies in movie theaters. I don’t really remember when that happened, but it happened sometime after I came back home. While I was in grad school, I remember going out to see matinees at the movie theater in Auburn.

I have a long-running joke with a friend about “participating.” He and his wife are real movie fans, sort of hardcore movie fans really. When we were chatting once years ago, he asked if I’d seen this movie or that movie — films that were then currently in the theaters. I said “no” to each and ended with “I really don’t participate in the culture.”

If he asks these days if I’ve seen a current movie, heard a current song, or read a current book, I still say, “I really don’t participate in the culture,” much to his consternation.

I was joking the first time I said it, but I don’t think I am anymore.

More than a few times, I’ve confessed my ignorance of current popular culture here; there are some random things I know, but not many. Last night, for example, I watched the last of “Cobra Kai” on Netflix. However, that franchise started with the 1984 move “The Karate Kid,” which I still think is one of the best movies of the 1980s.

By the way, overall I was pleased with how “Cobra Kai” unfolded over the years, and the finale wasn’t bad at all! Enjoyable!

And there it is … all packed into one series the reasons that I don’t participate in the culture.

First, although Netflix is raising its prices soon, it will still be less expensive for a month of programming than going to the movies once, by myself, and getting popcorn. Seriously. Streaming services like Netflix feature recent movies, so I can participate in the culture all I want if I have a little patience.

Second, I’m growing increasingly uncomfortable with seeing movies in theaters. It’s not the people. It’s the simple fact that I cannot stop the movie when I want to for “comfort breaks.” They keep making movies longer and longer, which is fine, but if I’m spending the last half-hour fidgeting because I need a bathroom break, I’m not paying attention.

There are a lot of classic movies that are really long, but most of them have intermissions. Bringing back longer run times for movies is one thing, but why they haven’t brought back intermissions is another thing entirely.

Third, I simply don’t care about the nearly baroque level of stories being told in current movies. In 2023, I had seen two of the Best Picture nominees — “Oppenheimer” in the theater, and “Barbie” on streaming. “Oppenheimer” won Best Picture, but I found “Barbie” to be far more entertaining. However, it would have been difficult for me to make a choice between the two for Best Picture, so I’m not going to quibble about it.

Best Supporting Actor was also an interesting category; Robert Downey, Jr. won for his part in “Oppenheimer” … and he should have. He was brilliant. So was Ryan Gosling in “Barbie.” However, those movies simply weren’t comparable, in my mind. The Golden Globes may have it right with splitting the categories between drama and comedy.

However, the real snub that year was the fact that “Godzilla Minus One” wasn’t nominated for Best Picture. In my mind, that was the Best Picture of 2023.

See! That’s why I shouldn’t make Oscar picks or predictions. I don’t participate in the culture enough to know what’s going on. However, at least I saw some movies year-before-last. This last year, I just saw the one nominee.

Perhaps my non-participation in the culture has become self-fulfilling — I say I don’t, so I don’t. I don’t know. What I do know for sure is that I’m not particularly tempted to go to a movie theater these days. Since about 2020, I’ve only visited a movie theater when a friend has invited me. I go for the friend, not the movie.

I do participate in my friendships.

search_result.title: Who has the most Oscars? Top record-holders in Academy Awards ...
search_result.text:
March 3, 2023 Updated Jan. 24, 2024, 3:43 p.m. ET

Since 1929, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has awarded those in the entertainment industry for their significant achievements. The Academy Awards, or Oscars, is the oldest worldwide entertainment awards ceremony.

Each year, actors, actresses, directors, writers and more vie for the chance to win an Oscar. This year's ceremony, the 96th Academy Awards, will take place on Sunday, March 10, with nominees including "Oppenheimer", "Killers of the Flower Moon" and "Barbie".

In preparation for the biggest night in film, here are the top record holders for the most Academy Awards, including which actor, actress and films have won the most Oscars.

What is an Oscar?

An Oscar, or Academy Award, is presented annually by the film academy. The awards are given for "outstanding individual or collective film achievements in a wide variety of categories."

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

For the 96th Academy Awards, there are 23 categories, including best picture, best director, best actor and best actress.

The nominees are selected by academy members. Most categories are decided by members of the corresponding branches: actors nominate actors, directors nominate directors and so on.

From there, members vote online, and the results are announced on awards night. The votes are certified by PricewaterhouseCoopers, which has counted for the Oscars since 1935.

To be eligible for an Oscar, there are certain rules that must be fulfilled. The rules for the 96th Academy Awards can be found here.

This award is one-fourth of the coveted EGOT – signifying a person who has won an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar and Tony, the top awards in television, music, film and theater, respectively.

Who has the most Oscars?

Walt Disney holds the record for the most Oscars won by an individual with a total of 22 competitive awards and four honorary awards. Disney was nominated 59 times throughout his career, receiving one award posthumously.

He won the best short subject (cartoon) category 12 times, the best documentary (feature) category twice, the best documentary (short subject) category twice, the best short subject (live action) category once and the best short subject (two-reel) category five times.

Disney also received four special Oscars:

1933: For the creation of Mickey Mouse

1939: For "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs" and pioneering the motion picture cartoon

1942: For an "outstanding contribution to the advancement of the use of sound in motion pictures through the production of 'Fantasia ' "

1942: The Irving G. Thalberg Memorial Award

What actor has the most Oscars?

There is a three-way tie for the male actor with the most Oscars. Walter Brennan, Daniel Day-Lewis and Jack Nicholson all have three awards.

Brennan won best supporting actor three times, the only performer to do so. He won during the:

9th Academy Awards for his performance as Swan Bostrom in "Come and Get It"

11th Academy Awards for his performance as Peter Goodwin in "Kentucky"

13th Academy Awards for his performance as Judge Roy Bean in "The Westerner"

Day-Lewis won best actor three times. He is the only performer to win three Oscars in this category. He won during the:

62nd Academy Awards for his performance as Christy Brown in "My Left Foot"

80th Academy Awards for his performance as Daniel Plainview in "There Will Be Blood"

85th Academy Awards for his performance as Abraham Lincoln in "Lincoln"

Nicholson has won best actor twice and best supporting actor once. He won during the:

48th Academy Awards for his performance as Randle Patrick McMurphy in "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest"

56th Academy Awards for his performance as Garrett Breedlove in "Terms of Endearment"

70th Academy Awards for his performance as Melvin Udall in "As Good As It Gets"

The actress with the most Oscars is Katharine Hepburn, who won best actress four times. Hepburn is the only performer to win four competitive acting Oscars. She won during the:

6th Academy Awards for her performance as Eva Lovelace in "Morning Glory"

40th Academy Awards for her performance as Christina Drayton in "Guess Who's Coming to Dinner"

41st Academy Awards for her performance as Queen Eleanor of Aquitaine in "The Lion in Winter"

54th Academy Awards for her performance as Ethel Thayer in "On Golden Pond"

Film with the most Oscars

Three films are tied for having the most Oscars. "Ben-Hur," "Titanic" and "The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King" all received 11 awards, respectively.

"Ben-Hur" received 11 awards out of 12 nominations during the 32nd Academy Awards. It won in the following categories:

Actor

Supporting actor

Art direction (color)

Cinematography (color)

Costume design (color)

Directing

Film editing

Music (music score of a drama or comedy)

Best picture

Sound

Special effects

"Titanic" received 11 awards out of 14 nominations during the 70th Academy Awards. It won in the following categories:

Art direction

Cinematography

Costume design

Directing

Film editing

Original dramatic score

Original song

Best picture

Sound

Sound effects editing

Visual effects

"The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King" received 11 awards out of 11 nominations during the 76th Academy Awards. It won in the following categories:

Art direction

Costume design

Directing

Film editing

Makeup

Original score

Original song

Best picture

Sound mixing

Visual effects

Adapted screenplay

Just Curious for more?

Here at Just Curious, we're looking into all of your questions. From "Who was the oldest Golden Girl?" to "Who has the most Grammys?" to information about all things Barbie. Make sure to check out USA TODAY's Just Curious section for more information you might want to know.

search_result.title: When did the Academy Awards start? A look at the first Oscar winners
search_result.text:
March 3, 2026 Updated March 9, 2026, 1:24 p.m. ET

Every year heading into the Oscars, the lead-up is chaos as studios seek momentum for their nominated movies, actors are the centerpiece of "For your consideration" campaigns, and pundits predict the big winners all the way up until showtime.

That sort of frazzled energy wasn't a part of the first Academy Awards: The ceremony took place three months after the winners were announced.

Academy President Douglas Fairbanks emceed the event on May 16, 1929, where gold statuettes – designed a year earlier by MGM’s art director, Cedric Gibbons – were handed out in 12 competitive categories (plus two special awards) honoring the best films of 1927 and 1928.

With the 98th Academy Awards less than two weeks away on March 15, here's what else movie lovers need to know about the first Oscars:

Join our Watch Party! Sign up to receive USA TODAY's movie and TV recommendations right in your inbox

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Where were the first Oscars held?

Presented at the Blossom Room in Los Angeles' Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel, the first Academy Awards was more dinner party than today's high-profile spectacle, with 270 in attendance and guest tickets priced at $5 apiece. (In comparison, a movie ticket cost a quarter.) It was also the only Oscar ceremony that wasn't broadcast in some way: Because of the 1929 banquet's popularity, a local radio station did a live hourlong broadcast at the second Oscars on April 3, 1930.

What movie won best picture at the first Oscars?

"Wings," a drama starring Charles "Buddy" Rogers and Richard Arlen as World War I fighter pilots and Clara Bow as the love interest of both men, won "outstanding picture" (which would ultimately be renamed best picture) as well as best engineering effects. It was also the only silent film to ever win the top category.

"Sunrise," F.W. Murnau's film about a city woman (Margaret Livingston) who tries to persuade a farmer (George O'Brien) to murder his wife (Janet Gaynor), tied for the most Oscars (three) with the romance "7th Heaven." Most notably "Sunrise" won best unique and artistic picture, an award that was scrapped a year later when the Oscars winnowed its categories to seven.

Fun fact: You can stream both "Wings" and "Sunrise" on Tubi.

Who won the first best actor and best actress Academy Awards?

In the first year of the Oscars, winners could be honored for single movies, multiple films, or a body of work. Janet Gaynor won best actress for her roles in "Sunrise," "7th Heaven" and "Street Angel" – in 1938, she was again nominated in the category for the first "A Star Is Born."

Best actor went to Swiss-German performer Emil Jannings for "The Last Command" and "The Way of All Flesh." Jannings later starred in 1930's "The Blue Angel" with Marlene Dietrich but never acted again in Hollywood after World War II because of his work in Nazi propaganda films.

What other movies won at the first Oscars?

Two best directing Oscars were handed out in 1929 – Lewis Milestone won for the comedy picture "Two Arabian Nights" while Frank Borzage got a trophy for "7th Heaven."

Interestingly, two influential movies of the time received honorary Oscars. Charlie Chaplin was originally going to be nominated for best actor, director, writing and outstanding picture for "The Circus," but the Academy Board of Judges unanimously decided to remove his name from the competitive categories and instead bestow him with one special award for "versatility and genius in writing, acting, directing and producing."

"The collective accomplishments thus displayed place you in a class by yourself," the Academy wrote in a letter to Chaplin dated Feb. 19, 1929.

Three months later, when presenter William C. de Mille accepted the award on the star's behalf, he told the attendees that Chaplin had "cold feet" and skipped the ceremony, "but he has wired his high appreciation for the honor."

The other special award went to Warner Bros. for producing 1927's "The Jazz Singer," widely considered the first "talkie."

search_result.title: Shockingly, these A-list movie stars have never won an Oscar
search_result.text:
March 13, 2026, 7:30 a.m. ET

Oscar has had a date with many of Hollywood's biggest stars since the first Academy Awards in 1929.

But you might be surprised which movie icons have yet to wrap their fingers around that vaunted golden statue. Eddie Murphy has been entertaining millions since he was a teen; no Oscar. Harrison Ford, who received a moving Actor Awards tribute earlier this month, is synonymous with the popcorn thriller. But still, no Academy Award.

The reasons for Oscar slights are often as simple as an A-list actor being in a particularly competitive field. Other times, an upstart rushes in on the heels of a popular film and steals the night.

For example, in 2015, notable nominees such as Bradley Cooper, Michael Keaton, Benedict Cumberbatch and Steve Carell all lost to Eddie Redmayne, who won best actor for his portrayal of Stephen Hawking in "The Theory of Everything." And in 2023, Cate Blanchett and Michelle Williams stayed seated as the Oscar went to "Everything Everywhere All at Once" darling Michelle Yeoh.

Take a spin with us through this list of favorite actors who have yet to win an Oscar heading into the 2026 Academy Awards on March 15 (ABC and Hulu, 7 p.m. ET/4 PT).

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

USA TODAY Movie Meter: Help select the film of the year!

Join our Watch Party! Sign up to receive USA TODAY's movie and TV recommendations right in your inbox

Ethan Hawke

The journeyman actor has been a consistent Hollywood presence for decades, with memorable turns in “Dead Poets Society,” “Reality Bites,” “First Reformed” and “Before the Devil Knows You’re Dead.” He now has five Oscar nominations: two for best adapted screenplay, for “Before Sunset” and “Before Midnight,” and three for acting in “Training Day,” “Boyhood” and this year’s music biopic “Blue Moon.” But the Texas native has yet to clinch a trophy of his own.

Stellan Skarsgård

The Swedish veteran has juggled serious dramas ("Breaking the Waves," "Good Will Hunting") with musicals ("Mamma Mia!"), thrillers ("The Girl With the Dragon Tattoo") and popcorn fare ("Dune"). But the "Dogville" actor has never won an Oscar, and only earned his first nomination this year for the Norwegian family dramedy "Sentimental Value."

Ralph Fiennes

You'd probably lose the bet if asked whether Ralph Fiennes has an Oscar. And yet, the British thespian has never stood before Hollywood's finest and recited an acceptance speech, despite riveting work in movies such as "Schindler's List" (best supporting actor nominee) and "The English Patient" (best actor nominee). Last year, he was back in the big leagues for his best actor-nominated turn as a conflicted but incorruptible Catholic cardinal in the papal thriller "Conclave."

Cynthia Erivo

Cynthia Erivo has had her Oscar hopes raised thrice before, when she garnered nominations in 2020 for best actress and best original song for "Harriet," the story of abolitionist leader Harriet Tubman. And last season, the British star was again up for best actress for her indelible performance as Elphaba, aka the Wicked Witch of the West, in "Wicked."

Edward Norton

Edward Norton has garnered four Oscar nominations spanning decades, from 1997 to 2025. While his work has won him a Golden Globe and Screen Actors Guild award, Oscar has remained elusive. He most recently netted a best supporting actor nomination for playing folk legend Pete Seeger in the Bob Dylan biopic "A Complete Unknown."

Isabella Rossellini

Isabella Rossellini is the daughter of genuine Hollywood royalty. Her father was famed Italian director Roberto Rossellini, whose marriage to iconic Hollywood star Ingrid Bergman was the biggest scandal of its day because of Bergman leaving her husband and child for the director. Isabella Rossellini went into the family business, turning in an especially captivating performance in David Lynch's 1986 creep-fest, "Blue Velvet." She finally received her first Oscar nomination last year for papal thriller "Conclave."

Angela Bassett

In 1994, Angela Bassett was nominated for best actress for her powerful turn as Tina Turner in “What’s Love Got to Do With It.” Three decades later, she was up for an Oscar for best supporting actress as Queen Ramonda in "Black Panther: Wakanda Forever." The award went to Jamie Lee Curtis for "Everything Everywhere All at Once," but in 2024, Bassett did take home her prized statue: She received an honorary Oscar.

Annette Bening

A five-time Oscar nominee, Annette Bening, who is married to Hollywood icon Warren Beatty, has yet to win. She put in an especially fierce, raw performance in 2023's "Nyad," in which she played real-life open water swimming legend Diana Nyad (opposite Oscar winner Jodie Foster). Bening got in such ferocious shape for the movie that she shot scenes for hours at a time, never leaving the water. But alas, no Oscar yet.

Bradley Cooper

Since 2013, Bradley Cooper has been nominated for 12 Oscars, including five as an actor: “Silver Linings Playbook,” “American Hustle,” “American Sniper," "A Star Is Born" and "Maestro," in which he transformed into conductor Leonard Bernstein. But he's empty-handed so far after losing to Cillian Murphy's performance as American physicist J. Robert Oppenheimer in 2024.

Paul Giamatti

Paul Giamatti has seemingly been on our screens large and small forever, yet he has yet to take home significant hardware for his consistently compelling performances, whether that's as a wine snob ("Sideways") or a vengeful New York attorney general ("Billions"). While Giamatti got some Oscar nomination love in 2006 for the boxing movie "Cinderella Man" and in 2024 for his performance as a curmudgeonly boarding school professor in "The Holdovers," he has not taken home Hollywood's most coveted trophy.

Mark Ruffalo

In the last dozen years, Mark Ruffalo has been up for four acting Oscars. So far, he hasn’t had the opportunity to give an acceptance speech at the Academy Awards. His best supporting actor nomination for "Poor Things" gave him his most recent shot at the gold, but the veteran and versatile actor is still looking for the role that will get him in the game once more.

Colin Farrell

Colin Farrell first stood out two decades ago in his star turn opposite Tom Cruise in 2002's sci-fi thriller "Minority Report." He's continued to dazzle ever since in everything from black comedies ("In Bruges") to TV reboots ("Miami Vice"). He received his first Oscar nomination for his subtle performance in 2022's "The Banshees of Inisherin," but the award went to Brendan Fraser for "The Whale."

Michelle Williams

Michelle Williams has five Oscar acting nominations, but her performances to date have gone unrewarded by the Academy. In 2023, she was up for best actress for playing a character based on Steven Spielberg's mother in his memoir of a film, "The Fabelmans." She lost to Michelle Yeoh.

Scarlett Johansson

Scarlett Johansson's two nominations in 2019 – for best actress in "Marriage Story" and supporting actress in "Jojo Rabbit" – so far have been the A-list actress' only appearances on Oscar's radar.

Antonio Banderas

Spanish heartthrob Antonio Banderas was nominated for the first time for a best actor Oscar for his 2019 role in "Pain and Glory." He also has been nominated for five Golden Globes without a win.

Saoirse Ronan

Saoirse Ronan's turn in "Little Women" brought the actress to the Oscar nominations list for a fourth time. She was also nominated for "Atonement," "Brooklyn" and "Lady Bird."

Glenn Close

Glenn Close has been nominated a whopping eight times, including for best actress in 2018's “The Wife.” She's widely considered to be one of the best actresses to never have won an Oscar.

Willem Dafoe

Why, yes, Willem Dafoe was nominated for an Academy Award a few years back for “At Eternity’s Gate.” And nominated again the year before that, and two other times as well. He seems long overdue, but was overlooked for a nomination in 2024 for "Poor Things."

Amy Adams

Amy Adams, who was recognized in 2024 for her spirited performance in "Nightbitch" with Golden Globe and Spirit Award nominations, has nevertheless heard another name called in her Oscar acting category six times. She’s also been nominated for seven BAFTAs, but never won one of those, either.

Sam Elliott

Sam Elliott, who made his film debut in 1969 in “Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid,” was nominated for his first Oscar in 2019 for supporting actor in “A Star Is Born.” But he didn't win.

Viggo Mortensen

Viggo Mortensen lost in his bid for an Oscar in 2019 for best picture winner “Green Book,” after losing in the actor category twice previously.

Michelle Pfeiffer

Michelle Pfeiffer is someone you probably assume has won an Oscar before. She’s been in the running for three awards, but hasn’t won any yet. But she's still happily on the small screen, most recently with Felicity Jones in the streaming holiday comedy "Oh. What. Fun."

Tom Cruise

Tom Cruise seems to embody The Movies. Decades of films, many of them hits, have made him an ubiquitous presence on the big screen, including his magnetic performance in "Top Gun" and its sequel "Top Gun: Maverick." Though "Maverick" was in contention for best picture, Cruise wasn't nominated for his title role, even after three previous acting nominations. For some consolation, he received an honorary Oscar last November.

Jake Gyllenhaal

Though he continues to prove he’s worthy of awards recognition, Jake Gyllenhaal has never won an Academy Award. He’s been nominated once for supporting actor in “Brokeback Mountain.”

Samuel L. Jackson

Samuel L. Jackson also doesn't have an Oscar. He's been in more than 100 films but has been nominated just once, in 1995 for "Pulp Fiction." He received an honorary Oscar in 2022.

Sigourney Weaver

Sigourney Weaver has been nominated for three Academy Awards, but has never won. She riveted audiences in "Alien," and more recently played memorable roles in "Avatar" and its 2025 sequel.

Michael Fassbender

Irish actor Michael Fassbender has twice been in the running for an Oscar, both in 2014 as supporting actor in "12 Years A Slave" and in 2016 as best actor for his portrayal of Apple founder "Steve Jobs."

Sylvester Stallone

Sly Stallone charged out of the gates strong in 1976, thanks to his perfect screenplay and nuanced performance in "Rocky." But no Oscar for either. In 2016, the Academy tried once again to reward the action hero, this time with a supporting actor nod for the Rocky-derived film "Creed." But nothing.

Naomi Watts

Naomi Watts is one of those actors who demands attention when she's on the screen. Oscar nods came her way twice, in 2004 for "21 Grams" and 2013 for "The Impossible." She's only won one major award to date, a Screen Actors Guild cast award in 2015 for "Birdman."

Woody Harrelson

The former TV star (remember "Cheers"?) has been nominated for movie's highest honor three times, including for best actor for his role as pornographer Larry Flynt in 1996's "The People v. Larry Flynt." Harrelson has worked non-stop ever since, but Oscar remains elusive.

search_result.title: Oscars 2021: Get Academy Awards ready with 500 photos, trivia
search_result.text:
April 23, 2021 Updated April 25, 2021, 5:53 p.m. ET

Since the very first Academy Awards took place in 1929, plenty of movie history has been made at the Oscars.

And this year's 93rd Academy Awards — the first to take place during a pandemic — is sure to bring more unforgettable moments.

Following a pandemic-plagued year and the weirdest awards season ever, the Oscars return this Sunday (ABC, 8 p.m. ET/5 PT). Though the show is bound to look different due to the pandemic, some traditions, like a glam red carpet pre-show and in-person celebrity presenters, are still set to go on — albeit with under strict COVID-19 protocol.

To prepare for the big night, we've compiled a crash course on everything you could possibly want to know going into the ceremony. So sit back, relax and soak up some trivia that will make you the star of any virtual Oscars viewing party.

How to watch the 2021 Oscars: Everything you should know about Sunday's Academy Awards

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

We put it to the test: How helpful are the new Oscars diversity standards?

Best Oscars moments of all time

To kick things off, here's a round-up of some of the most memorable moments in Oscars history.

So who's nominated this year?

The competition is shaping up to be fierce at this year's Academy Awards. Get to know the top nominees, including the films "Mank" and "Minari," here:

Here's our take on who will win – and who should.

Every best picture nominee directed by a woman

For the first time in Oscars history, two women are nominated for best director in a single year. Prior to this year, only five women had been recognized in the prestigious category — even though 16 films directed by women had been up for best picture. Here are all of the best pictures nominees directed by women.

Oscar producers talk unprecedented show amid COVID, the 'teeny' red carpet and the role of masks

Where are the Oscars this year?

The show is getting a change of scenery this year. Here's a glimpse of Los Angeles' historic Union Station, which will serve as the main venue of the 93rd Academy Awards instead of the Dolby Theater. You might even recognize the train station from classic Old Hollywood films.

Oscars at a train station? Why the Academy Awards at Union Station isn't weird, it's movie magic

Every best picture winner ever

It's the most anticipated (and often most controversial) award of the night. Here's every film that's taken home best picture.

After chaos and a pandemic, will history regard this year’s Oscar winners differently?

But not every best picture is actually the best ...

Oscars history is full of surprising snubs. Here's a list of best picture winners that didn't deserve it, along with the nominee that should have taken home the trophy instead.

In fact, some of the best films of all time have never won an Oscar

Believe it or not, "It's a Wonderful Life," "Psycho" and other classics that are considered the best in film history were never awarded Oscars.

So, who's the best of the best?

Don't have time to binge all 92 best picture winners before Sunday? We don't blame you. Here are 10 winning films that are the best of the best pictures.

And who's the worst of the worst?

Just because a film won best picture doesn't mean it's actually good. Here are 10 less-than-stellar films that took home the top prize, ranked from bad to downright awful.

Flashback: Take a glance at Oscars past

Plenty of icons have graced the Academy Awards. Relive old Hollywood glamour with this gallery of stars at Oscars past.

The best Oscars looks of the '90s

Embrace your inner '90s kid and admire the best Oscars looks from the era of floppy disks.

The best Oscars looks of all time...

Perhaps just as anticipated as the awards ceremony is the red carpet fashion that precedes it. Here are the most glam Academy Awards looks of all time.

... and the worst

While many gowns wow on the red carpet, some stun for all the wrong reasons. Here are the worst Oscars looks of all time.

Winners with winning style

When you go onstage to accept an Academy Award, the right dress can really make you shine. Here are some A-plus looks from A-list winners.

What goes down behind the scenes?

Wait, a cocktail at the bar costs how much? Here are 10 things you wouldn't know about the Oscars if you've only watched from home.

Times the Oscars got political

The Oscars have become known as an event where stars can speak their mind, and these celebs definitely made a statement with their fiery political speeches.

And the award for most unforgettable mishap in Oscars history goes to ...

It wouldn't be a live TV event if everything went according to plan.

Relive the jaw-dropping moment when "La La Land" was accidentally declared the best picture winner in 2017 – only for it come out shortly after that "Moonlight" had actually earned the honor.

search_result.title: Oscars 2017: 7 things to look for at this year's Academy Awards
search_result.text:
Chris Foran

Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Updated Feb. 23, 2017, 1:03 p.m. ET

It's "La La Land's" world at the Oscars this year — unless it's not.

Since Jan. 24, when the nominations for this year's Academy Awards were announced, the musical has been christened as the front-runner. "La La Land's" 14 nominations tied the record set by 1950's "All About Eve" and equaled by 1997's "Titanic."

Since then, the movie has done nothing but rack up awards: best film at the Producers Guild Awards, best film at the BAFTA Awards, Britain's Oscars, plus a slew of awards for director and screenwriter Damien Chazelle, composer (and Nicolet High School grad) Justin Hurwitz, star Emma Stone, and in many of the technical categories.

The Oscars' top category is called "best picture," not "best movie," and historically, that's been for a reason. In nearly every year since the start of the Oscars in 1929, the award for best picture has gone to the movie that makes Hollywood look smartest, most ambitious, most creative and most forward-thinking. "La La Land" is exactly the kind of movie the Academy loves to honor: bold, engaging and a sign that Hollywood can do unexpected things — while, of course, celebrating movie-making in the process.

All signs — including the oddsmakers' payouts — are that "La La Land" is a sure thing for best picture.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

But, as we have seen elsewhere in 2017, this isn't just any year.

While "La La Land" has been collecting trophies, something else has been going on in Hollywood — a vocal backlash against just about everything associated with the Trump administration.

"La La Land" is many things, but a political statement it ain't. It's possible that Academy voters have decided to look for a protest pick for best picture. "Hidden Figures," "Moonlight" and "Lion," which in very different ways smartly and compellingly address issues of race, identity, gender and poverty, could also earn votes as representatives of historically voiceless communities.

So, while the house is still betting on "La La Land," Hollywood could deal a wild card.

Related:Where and how you can watch this year's Oscar-nominated movies

Here are some other things to look for at this year's Oscars, airing Sunday at 7:30 p.m. on ABC.

Back in tune

If "La La Land" wins for best picture, it'll be the first time a musical has won in that category since "Chicago" in 2003. Before "La La Land," the last time a musical with original music and story received a best picture nomination was 1979's "All That Jazz."

Veterans welcome

While seven of this year's 20 acting nominees are first-timers, six of the nominees have won Oscars before, including Denzel Washington with two and Meryl Streep with three.

Ceiling crashers

"La La Land's" Ai-Ling Lee and Mildred Iatrou Morgan are the first female team to be nominated for an Oscar for best sound editing. Joi McMillon, who shares a film editing nomination with Nat Sanders for "Moonlight," is the first African-American woman, and just the second African-American, ever nominated in the category.

#OscarsNotSoWhite, Part 1

After last year's Academy Awards, in which none of the 20 acting nominations went to people of color, seven people of color were nominated for supporting or leading actor Oscars this year. While people of color are still substantially underrepresented onscreen among this year's nominated films, four of the nine best picture nominees centered on stories about people of color: "Fences," "Hidden Figures," "Lion" and "Moonlight."

#OscarsNotSoWhite, Part 2

Only three times in the Academy's history, two of the four acting Oscars have gone to black performers: 2002 (Halle Berry, Denzel Washington), 2005 (Jamie Foxx, Morgan Freeman), and 2007 (Forest Whitaker, Jennifer Hudson). This year, Viola Davis ("Fences") and Mahershala Ali ("Moonlight") are favorites in the supporting categories; Washington, in a neck-and-neck battle with Casey Affleck ("Manchester by the Sea") for the best actor prize, could make it three — and make it a historic night.

Wisconsin connections

Besides Nicolet High School grad Justin Hurwitz — nominated for original score and sharing two nominations for original song for "La La Land" — Wisconsin will have some indirect representation at Sunday's Oscars. Sting's Oscar-nominated song "Empty Chair" is from "Jim: The James Foley Story," a documentary about the life and ideals of journalist and Marquette University grad James Foley. And, while he's not nominated, Neenah native Phil Johnston has a story and screenplay credit on "Zootopia," the favorite to win for best animated feature.

A late night?

The Academy Awards always seem to run late, but a big reason is the Oscars ceremony starts a half-hour later — at 7:30 p.m. Central time, to give ABC a slice of the red-carpet action — than the Grammys, Emmys or Tonys. But unlike the Grammys, which always says it will run 3½ hours and runs 3 hours, 35 minutes, the Oscars' producers, year in and year out, swear the show will run for three hours, and routinely the show runs for about 3 hours, 35 minutes.With the likely proliferation of political speeches, don't count on an early night.

search_result.title: Behind Oscar winners are casting directors. Soon we'll win Oscars, too
search_result.text:
Jen Rudin

Opinion contributor

March 10, 2024, 5:08 a.m. ET

Casting directors recently achieved a huge victory when the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences finally added a new Oscar category of casting for films released in 2025 and beyond.

The first Oscar ceremony was in 1929. The casting director job was created in the 1940s as the Hollywood studio system began dismantling and producers were no longer forced to hire actors on contracts at their studios. The first casting director received on-screen credit in 1972.

So why did it take the academy almost a hundred years to finally recognize our work?

“The casting director is your first advocate,” Oscar winner Marcia Gay Harden told me. “And God knows we need many advocates in this business. Casting directors are the first to stand behind you and help push you forward, which is hugely important to an actor.”

Alan Cumming added: “It's great that the academy is finally acknowledging how essential and integral a casting director's work is. We only think about casting when it’s not working so well, and that is a testament to the nuance, care, and dedication to character and story that this group of the film community has delivered so often and for so long. Now is their time to be celebrated and for their work to be given its rightful place in the creative process.”

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Now, casting directors could vote for Oscar winners but not win Oscars

The casting category is the first new one the academy has added since 2001. Marion Dougherty, a legendary casting director and the subject of Tom Donahue’s influential documentary "Casting By," had advocated for this category since the early 1990s. When "Casting By" premiered at the Toronto International Film Festival in 2012, it lit the fire to continue the push.

In 2013, the academy added a branch for casting directors to vote. This was a precarious position: We could vote but not win. It would take another decade to get this new category added.

As a child actor in the 1980s, legendary casting director Marcia Shulman gave me my first big break when she cast me in an off-Broadway play when I was 11. By age 12, I had my first epiphany, peering at the casting director from behind my signature purple glasses at a final callback for a TV movie.

She had identified two groups of kids to present to the ABC executives and the director. As the creative team deliberated over which group to choose, the casting director mediated their conversations and facilitated a calm experience – showing me that the job is a mix of digging deep to discover talent, populating the director’s vision by finding the actors to bring the story to life while navigating lots of egos and personalities. Mix in diplomacy and a photographic memory, too.

'Admire the world the casting director has built'

Having spent the past two decades as a casting director for film and TV, I've learned it’s a behind-the-scenes job that takes place long before the cameras roll.

Casting is an integral part of the film, like the cinematography, directing, costumes and sound.

Working with the Academy Award-nominated director Peter Bogdanovich to cast his final film, "She’s Funny That Way" from 2014, I was often the middle person communicating between him and the producers on deadline to hire actors and get everyone to set to make the best possible film.

You feel like a bartender, providing choices to the creative team. But even if you know the best option, you must let the director feel they have made the decision. Your role is to bring their vision to life.

“When you see a film, and you see the actors the casting directors have curated – the actors they’ve discovered, the risks that they’ve encouraged a director to often take – a penny drops about this other essential element of filmmaking,” Marcia Gay Harden noted. “Next time you watch a film, admire the world the casting director has built.”

When you watch the Oscars awards show Sunday, as we celebrate nominated performances, acknowledge the work of Ellen Lewis and Rene Haynes, who cast Lily Gladstone in "Killers of the Flower Moon,"and Susan Shopmaker (one of my casting mentors), who discovered Paul Giamatti’s young co-star Dominic Sessa in "The Holdovers."

After decades of demanding recognition for our craft, we will finally be on the red carpet at the Oscars in 2026, our turn to win.

Jen Rudin is the author of "Confessions of a Casting Director: Help Actors Land Any Role with Secrets from Inside the Audition Room," published by HarperCollins, and is the head of voice-over/animation at IAG.

search_result.title: Here's every film that won the Oscar for best picture - CNN
search_result.text: "It Happened One Night" (1935): "It Happened One Night" was one of the great underdog winners. Its studio, Columbia, wasn't considered one of the majors at the time, and neither Clark Gable nor Claudette Colbert, its stars, were excited about the project. But it became the first film to sweep the five major categories of picture, actor, actress, director and screenplay. To this day, only two other films -- "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest" (1975) and "The Silence of the Lambs" (1991) -- have pulled off the same trick. Columbia Pictures Corporation
